schools.summary: Paul addresses the case of 'a man overtaken (prolemphthe — caught, surprised, taken unawares) in a fault (paraptoma — a trespass, a sin)' — one fallen, perhaps by surprise of temptation rather than deliberate purpose; the charge to the 'spiritual' (the mature, Spirit-led believers): 'restore (katartizo — mend, set right, as a dislocated limb or a torn net) such an one in the spirit of meekness (gentleness)' — the fallen is to be tenderly recovered, not harshly condemned; 'considering thyself, lest thou also be tempted' (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry): the restorer is to consider himself — his own weakness and liability to fall — and so deal gently, knowing he too could be tempted; restoration, not punishment, is the goal.

themes: if a man be overtaken in a fault, ye which are spiritual, restore such an one in the spirit of meekness
considering thyself, lest thou also be tempted
the gentle restoring of the fallen
meekness and self-watch

schools.summary: Paul's charge: 'bear (carry, help to support) one another's burdens (barē — the heavy loads, the trials, sorrows, infirmities, and even faults that weigh believers down)' — the strong helping the weak, each sharing the other's load; to do so is to 'so fulfil the law of Christ' — the law of love (John 13:34, the new commandment), Christ's whole law being summed up in this mutual, self-giving love (Barnes, Robertson). Note: the 'burdens' (barē) here are distinguished from the 'load' (phortion) each must bear alone in v.5 — there is no contradiction.

Luther (with Calvin): over against the law of Moses, which the Judaizers pressed, stands 'the law of Christ' — love bearing the brother's burden; this, not circumcision, is the true fulfilling of the law.

themes: Bear ye one another's burdens, and so fulfil the law of Christ
the call to bear one another's burdens
the law of Christ is love
mutual self-giving love

schools.summary: Paul's caution against the pride that hinders burden-bearing: 'if a man think himself to be something (important, superior, above helping others) when he is nothing, he deceiveth (phrenapata — deludes) himself' — the conceited man is self-deceived; in himself, before God, the man is 'nothing' — without grace he has no worth or merit, and his high opinion of himself is a delusion (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): pride and self-conceit are what keep men from the lowly work of bearing others' burdens; the proud man, thinking himself above his brethren, deceives himself and fails in love.

themes: if a man think himself to be something, when he is nothing, he deceiveth himself
the warning against self-conceit
pride hindering burden-bearing
self-conceit as self-deception

schools.summary: Paul's direction against conceited comparison: 'let every man prove (dokimazo — test, examine, as assaying metal) his own work' — judge his own conduct by the proper, absolute standard, not by comparison with others; then his 'rejoicing (ground of boasting)' will be 'in himself alone (with reference to his own actual character) and not in another' — not in feeling superior to a worse neighbour, but in the honest worth of his own work before God (Barnes, Robertson).

Wesley (with Calvin): true ground for rejoicing is found only in an approving conscience, the honest discharge of one's own duty — never in the false comfort of comparing oneself favourably with others.

themes: let every man prove his own work
rejoicing in himself alone, and not in another
testing one's own work, not comparing
an approving conscience the ground of rejoicing

schools.summary: The 'burden (phortion — a load, as a soldier's pack or a ship's cargo)' here is different from the 'burdens (barē)' of v.2; this is the load of personal responsibility — each must answer to God for his own actions, a responsibility that cannot be shifted onto another. There is no contradiction with v.2 ('bear ye one another's burdens'): there, believers are to share each other's trials and infirmities (barē); here, each must bear alone the phortion of his own accountability before God's judgment — sympathy in trials, but no transfer of responsibility (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): at the last judgment each will stand alone and bear his own burden; the praises of others will not help, but the witness of his own conscience will stand for or against him.

debates.question: Does v.5 ('bear his own burden') contradict v.2 ('bear one another's burdens')?

sides.position: No contradiction — v.2 (barē) is sharing others' trials/infirmities; v.5 (phortion) is one's own accountability before God, which none can shift

themes: every man shall bear his own burden
each bearing his own load before God
phortion vs barē — no contradiction with v.2
the individuality of judgment

schools.summary: Paul's direction: 'let him that is taught (the learner, the catechized) in the word communicate (koinoneito — share, give a share of his good things) unto him that teacheth (the teacher, the minister) in all good things' — the taught are to provide for the temporal support of their teachers; 'all good things' meaning especially material support, the people sharing their temporal goods with those who minister to them in spiritual things (as in 1 Corinthians 9:11); the laborer is worthy of his support (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Henry, Calvin): it is the duty and privilege of those who receive spiritual instruction to maintain their teachers; ingratitude toward faithful ministers is a fault.

themes: Let him that is taught in the word communicate unto him that teacheth in all good things
the duty to support those who teach the word
sharing temporal goods with teachers
maintaining faithful ministers

schools.summary: Paul's solemn caution: 'be not deceived (do not delude yourselves); God is not mocked (mukterizetai — cannot be sneered at, outwitted, or evaded)' — none can cheat God or escape his justice by pretence; the fixed law: 'whatsoever a man soweth (the kind of seed, the course of life he pursues), that shall he also reap (the corresponding harvest)' — the harvest answers infallibly to the sowing; conduct has consequences God ensures (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): this applies to the support of teachers and to all conduct — those who sow stinginess or fleshly indulgence reap accordingly; God's justice cannot be evaded by hypocrisy.

themes: Be not deceived; God is not mocked
whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap
the law of sowing and reaping
God's justice inescapable

schools.summary: Gill: sowing to the flesh is not the lawful care of the body (seeking its health, providing food, raiment, and necessaries for one's family), but pampering and gratifying the lusts of the flesh, minding the things of the flesh; while sowing to the Spirit is acting under his influence and direction; Paul declaring that he who sows to his flesh shall of the flesh reap corruption, but he who sows to the Spirit shall of the Spirit reap life everlasting — the eternal contrast of the two harvests (Paul applying the law of sowing to the great choice of life: to live for the flesh, indulging its desires, yielding only corruption, ruin and death, while to live by the Spirit, in his ways and obedience, yielding the harvest of everlasting life, the two seeds bearing fruit each after its own kind into eternity).

themes: he that soweth to his flesh shall... reap corruption
he that soweth to the Spirit shall... reap life everlasting
the eternal contrast of the two harvests
each seed after its own kind

schools.summary: Gill: 'well doing' may mean good works of every sort (such as are done according to the will of God, from a principle of love, in faith, in the name and strength of Christ, and with a view to God's glory), or acts of beneficence to Christ's ministers and poor in particular; Paul exhorting them not to be weary in well doing, for in due season they shall reap if they faint not — the promise of a harvest to the persevering (Paul, lest the toil of doing good discourage, urging patient continuance, with the cheering assurance that the harvest is certain and will come in God's appointed time, so the only danger is in fainting and giving up before the reaping).

themes: let us not be weary in well doing
in due season we shall reap
if we faint not
the certain harvest to the persevering

schools.summary: Gill: 'as we have opportunity' (or ability) means as occasion requires, as objects offer, and as there is ability of well doing while the time of life lasts, which is the time for sowing or doing good works; Barnes: this is the true rule about doing good, the opportunity to do good imposing the obligation; Paul directing them to do good to all men as they have opportunity, especially to those of the household of faith — the wide reach and special priority of Christian benevolence (Paul setting the bounds of doing good as wide as opportunity and ability allow, reaching out to all men whatever their state, yet marking a special claim and priority for fellow-believers, the household of faith, who are to be the first and chief objects of the church's love and help).

themes: as we have therefore opportunity, let us do good unto all men
especially unto them who are of the household of faith
the wide reach of benevolence
special priority for fellow-believers

schools.summary: Gill: the phrase may be read 'see ye' (imperative) or 'ye see' (indicative), and 'how large a letter' (the length) or 'with what letters' (the size of the characters) — which some understand of the largeness of the characters he wrote, others of his unpracticed hand (being a Hebrew, not used to writing Greek); Paul calling attention to the large letter, or large characters, he had written with his own hand — the mark of personal love and earnestness (Paul, who often dictated and signed only the close, here pointing out that he wrote this letter, or much of it, with his own hand, the unusual labour a token of his deep love for the Galatians and his earnest concern for the weighty matter, that they might weigh the more what cost him such pains).

themes: ye see how large a letter
I have written unto you with mine own hand
the mark of personal love and earnestness
the unusual labour a token of concern

schools.summary: Gill: the false apostles desired to carry a good face of things outwardly, to appear outwardly righteous before men and be accounted so, doing all to be seen of them and gain their applause; Paul exposing that those who desire to make a fair show in the flesh constrain them to be circumcised, only to escape persecution for the cross of Christ — the cowardice and self-interest behind the false teaching (Paul laying bare the real motive of the Judaizers: not zeal for God but a craving for outward show and the desire to avoid the persecution that faithful preaching of the cross brings, for by pressing circumcision on the Gentiles they kept in with the Jews and shielded themselves from the offence of the cross).

themes: as many as desire to make a fair shew in the flesh
they constrain you to be circumcised
lest they should suffer persecution for the cross of Christ
the cowardice and self-interest of the false teachers

schools.summary: Gill: the pleaders for and preachers of circumcision (whether themselves actually circumcised or not, those on that side of the question, whether heartily or not) did not keep the law themselves; Barnes: it was true in general that they did not keep it; Paul declaring that even the circumcised do not keep the law themselves, yet desire to have the Galatians circumcised that they may glory in their flesh — the inconsistency and self-glorying of the Judaizers (Paul exposing a double fault: they who pressed the law on others did not keep it themselves, proving their insincerity, and their real aim in winning Gentile converts to circumcision was not the Galatians' good but their own boasting, that they might glory in the number they had made proselytes).

themes: neither they themselves who are circumcised keep the law
desire to have you circumcised
that they may glory in your flesh
the hypocrisy and vainglory of the Judaizers

schools.summary: Gill: Paul expresses his aversion to glorying in anything these men did — not in his outward carnal privileges as a Jew, nor in his moral, civil, and legal righteousness, nor in his gifts and attainments, nor in his labours and success as of himself, nor in the flesh of others, but in the cross of Christ; Paul declaring that he will glory in nothing but the cross of the Lord Jesus, by which the world is crucified to him and he to the world — the cross as the only boast and the great divorce from the world (Paul, against the Judaizers' glorying in the flesh, making the crucified Christ his one and only boast, and testifying that through that cross a mutual death has come between him and the world: the world, its honours, pleasures, and righteousness, dead and abhorrent to him as a crucified thing, and he dead to the world, its attractions and threats no longer holding him).

themes: God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ
the world is crucified unto me, and I unto the world
the cross as the only boast
the mutual death between Paul and the world

schools.summary: Gill: this gives a reason why Paul gloried in a crucified Christ and looked on the world as dead to him in every state of it, particularly the worldly sanctuary of the Jews and all its rites and ceremonies; circumcision and uncircumcision being of no account; Paul declaring that in Christ neither circumcision nor uncircumcision avails anything, but a new creature — the new creation as the one thing that counts (Paul, repeating the principle, sweeping aside the whole circumcision dispute as worthless in Christ, where outward marks count for nothing, and naming the one thing that truly avails: a 'new creature,' a new creation, the inward, regenerating work of the Spirit that makes a man over in Christ, which alone marks the true child of God).

themes: in Christ Jesus neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision
but a new creature
the new creation as the one thing that avails
the Spirit's regenerating work

schools.summary: Gill: the 'rule' (or canon) is not the canon of Scripture in general (the perfect rule of faith and practice) but the doctrine of justification by Christ's righteousness and the new creation, the truth Paul had been explaining, vindicating, and confirming; Paul pronouncing peace and mercy on as many as walk according to this rule, and upon the Israel of God — the benediction on the true people of God (Paul, having laid down the rule, the gospel of the new creation not circumcision, invoking peace and mercy on all who walk by it); the debate on 'the Israel of God': whether it adds a special blessing on believing Jews (the true Israel within the nation) or is another name for the whole church, Jew and Gentile, as the spiritual Israel, the true covenant people.

sides.position: The whole church, Jew and Gentile — the spiritual Israel, the true covenant people who walk by the gospel rule
Believing Jews specifically — the true Israel within the nation, given a special added blessing

themes: as many as walk according to this rule
peace be on them, and mercy
and upon the Israel of God
the benediction on the true people of God

schools.summary: Gill: having so clearly stated and proved that justification is not by works but by faith, and that circumcision and other rituals of the ceremonial law were not necessary to it, Paul in an authoritative way requires that they give him no further trouble on that head; Paul charging that henceforth no man trouble him, for he bears in his body the marks of the Lord Jesus — the badge of true apostleship in his scars (Paul, against the Judaizers who gloried in the mark of circumcision, pointing to the marks, the brands or stigmata, he bears in his own body, the scars of the beatings, stonings, and sufferings endured for Christ, as the true and only badge he needs, the visible proof that he belongs to and serves the Lord Jesus, before which the troublers must fall silent).

themes: from henceforth let no man trouble me
I bear in my body the marks of the Lord Jesus
the badge of true apostleship in his scars
the marks of suffering for Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Paul calls them 'brethren' to testify his affection for them, notwithstanding their infirmity and instability and the roughness with which he had treated them, and to show his great humility and condescension in owning the relation and putting them on a level with himself, which the pride of the false teachers would not suffer them to do; Paul closing the letter with the prayer that the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with their spirit, sealed with 'Amen' — the loving close in grace (Paul, who had dealt sharply with the Galatians for the truth's sake, ending the whole letter as a brother, in tenderness, invoking on them the very grace of Christ he had been defending, that it might be with their inmost spirit, a fitting and gracious seal to an epistle of grace against the law).

themes: brethren
the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit
the loving close in grace
ending as a brother, in tenderness
